chapter.title: Introduction and Objectives for Test Automation

list.item: TAE-1.1.1 (K2) Explain the advantages and disadvantages of test automation
TAE-1.2.1 (K2) Explain how test automation is applied across different SDLC models
TAE-1.2.2 (K2) Select suitable test automation tools for a given system under test

section.paragraph: Test automation includes using purpose-built tools to control and set up test suites, executing tests automatically, and comparing actual results to expected results. It covers many kinds of software: UI-based SUTs, headless systems, mobile apps, network protocols, and connections.

list.item: More tests per build compared to manual
Enables tests impossible to execute manually (real-time, remote, parallel)
Supports more complex tests than manual
Faster execution than manual tests
Less subject to human error
More effective and efficient resource use
Quicker feedback on SUT quality
Improves system reliability (availability, recoverability)
Improves consistency across test cycles
Additional costs: hiring TAEs, new hardware, training
Initial investment to set up the solution
Time to develop and maintain the solution
Requires clear objectives to ensure success
Rigidity of tests, less adaptable to SUT changes
Can introduce additional defects
Not all manual tests can be automated
Only verifies what automated tests are programmed to check
Can only check machine-interpretable results
Can only check results verifiable by an automated test oracle

section.paragraph: Linear and sequential with distinct phases (requirements, design, implementation, verification, maintenance). Test automation implementation typically happens in parallel to or after the implementation phase. Test runs usually take place during the verification phase.

Sequential process where test and integration activities are defined for each level of requirements. Traditional test levels: component, component integration, system, system integration, and acceptance. Providing a TAF for each test level is possible and recommended.

Countless possibilities for test automation. TAEs and business representatives decide on roadmap, timeline, and planned delivery. Best practices include code reviews, pair programming, and frequent automated test execution. Eliminating silos allows teams to cover all test levels with appropriate depth, achieving in-sprint automation.
To identify suitable tools, the SUT must first be analyzed. TAEs identify project requirements as the baseline for tool selection. Key considerations include:

list.item: Understand what the project wants to achieve over time (UI vs. web services)
Consider costs of commercial vs. open-source solutions
Evaluate team composition and programming experience
Low-code/no-code solutions for teams with limited programming experience
Match tool language to SUT language for better developer collaboration
Before automating anything, ask "should I?" — weigh the maintenance cost against the frequency of execution. A test run once a month manually may not justify automation overhead.
Match your tool to your SUT and team — if your team knows Python and the SUT is Python, pick a Python-native framework. Cross-training with devs becomes free.
Set clear automation objectives — define what success looks like before writing a single script (e.g., "reduce regression cycle from 3 days to 4 hours").
Don't automate the untestable — if a result can't be verified by an automated oracle (e.g., visual aesthetics), keep it manual or use visual regression tools.
In Agile, push for in-sprint automation — automate within the same sprint the feature is built. Delayed automation creates technical debt fast.
Track automation ROI monthly — count tests run, defects found, time saved vs. time spent maintaining. Present this to stakeholders regularly.
TAE-2.1.1 (K2) Describe configuration needs of an infrastructure for test automation
TAE-2.1.2 (K2) Explain how test automation is leveraged within different environments
TAE-2.2.1 (K4) Analyze a SUT to determine the appropriate test automation solution
TAE-2.2.2 (K4) Illustrate the technical findings of a tool evaluation

section.paragraph: Testability of the SUT (availability of software interfaces that support testing) should be designed and implemented in parallel with other SUT features. This is generally performed by a software architect, often with TAE involvement.

list.item: Accessibility identifiers — auto-generated by frameworks or set manually by developers
System environment variables — application parameters changed via administration for easier testing
Deployment variables — similar to system variables but set before deployment
Observability — SUT provides interfaces that give insight into its state, enabling result verification
Controllability — SUT provides interfaces for performing actions (UI elements, function calls, TCP/IP, USB protocols)
Architecture transparency — clear documentation of components and interfaces at all test levels

row.cell: Environment
Purpose
Component testing, GUI testing, API testing, white-box testing via IDE
Builds software, runs low-level tests and static analysis without deployment
Fully integrated SUT release candidate; black-box testing (UI/API); monitoring present
Non-functional testing (performance); resembles production; UAT by stakeholders; monitored
Real-time assessment with monitoring; canary releases, blue/green deployment, A/B testing

section.h2: 2.2 Evaluation Process for Tools and Strategies
section.paragraph: During SUT investigation, TAEs gather requirements considering scope and capabilities. Investigation is recommended in collaboration with stakeholders to identify risks and mitigations.

list.item: Which test process activities should be automated
Which test levels and test types should be supported
Which test roles and skill sets should be supported
Which software products and product lines should be supported
Which SUTs need to be compatible with the TAS
Availability and quality of test data
Methods to emulate unreachable cases (e.g., 3rd party applications)

section.paragraph: Capture findings in a comparison table (tools in columns, requirements in rows) to allow stakeholders to see differences.

list.item: Language/technology of the tool and IDE tools
Configurability: test environments, run configurations, dynamic/static setup
Test data management and version control integration
Support for different test types
Reporting capability aligned with project requirements
Integration with CI/CD, task tracking, test management tools
Scalability, maintainability, modifiability, compatibility, reliability
Advocate for testability early — push for data-test-id attributes, accessible APIs, and configurable environment variables during design reviews, not after.
Map your environments — document which test types run where (local = unit, integration = API/UI, preprod = performance). Avoid running expensive E2E tests in the wrong environment.
Build a tool comparison table before committing — list your top 3 candidates against 10+ criteria. Share it with stakeholders. This prevents "we picked it because it was popular" decisions.
Involve non-technical stakeholders in SUT analysis — business analysts and manual testers know the risks you'll miss from a purely technical perspective.
Check team skills before tool selection — a powerful tool is useless if nobody can maintain it. Low-code may be the right call for a team transitioning from manual testing.
Request monitoring in integration environments — without it, you'll spend hours chasing phantom defects that were actually environment issues.
TAE-3.1.1 (K2) Explain the major capabilities in a test automation architecture
TAE-3.1.2 (K2) Explain how to design a test automation solution
TAE-3.1.3 (K3) Apply layering of test automation frameworks
TAE-3.1.4 (K3) Apply different approaches for automating test cases
TAE-3.1.5 (K3) Apply design principles and design patterns in test automation

section.paragraph: The gTAA provides an abstract view of communication between test automation and connected systems (SUT, project management, test management, configuration management). It defines the capabilities necessary when designing a TAA.

list.item: SUT interface — connection between SUT and TAF
Project management interface — test automation development progress
Test management interface — mapping of test case definitions to automated test cases
Configuration management interface — CI/CD pipelines, environments, and testware

row.cell: Capability
Description
Automated design of test cases from a test model (optional; model-based testing tools)
Definition and implementation of test cases/suites, separated from SUT and tools
Test execution, logging, and reporting
Adaptors for connecting to SUT via APIs, protocols, and services

section.paragraph: A TAS is defined by functional, non-functional, and technical requirements of the SUT plus required tools. It is implemented with commercial or open-source tools and may need SUT-specific adaptors.

list.item: Selecting tools and tool-specific libraries
Developing plugins and/or components
Connectivity and interface requirements (firewalls, databases, URLs, mocks/stubs, message queues, protocols)
Connecting to test management and defect management tools
Utilizing version control systems and repositories

section.paragraph: The TAF is the foundation of a TAS, including a test harness (test runner) and test libraries. Layers define distinct boundaries of classes with similar purposes. Keep the number of layers low to avoid complicated design.

row.cell: Layer
Purpose
Test case repository and suite annotations; calls business logic layer; no direct calls to core libraries
SUT-dependent libraries; inherits from or uses facades of core libraries; TAF configuration
SUT-independent libraries; reusable across projects sharing the same development stack

key-box.heading: Scaling Test Automation
key-box.paragraph: Core libraries provide a reusable base for multiple TAFs. Different projects can build their TAFs on top of shared core libraries, enabling multiple TAEs to work independently on different projects while leveraging common infrastructure.

list.item: Initially easy to set up and use
Hard to maintain, scale, and evolve
SUT must be available during capture
Only feasible for small scope, rarely-changing SUTs
Recording each test case instead of reusing building blocks is time consuming
Easy to set up and start writing scripts
More modifiable than capture/playback
Hard to maintain, scale, and evolve
SUT must be available during capture
Some programming knowledge necessary
Easy to maintain, scale, port, adapt, and evolve
Business logic can be separated from test scripts
Programming knowledge necessary
Initial TAF development and testware definition is time consuming

card.heading: Test-Driven Development (TDD)
card.paragraph: Cycle: Red (write a failing test) → Green (implement minimal code to pass) → Refactor (optimize, apply clean code). User stories not verified via GUI/API testing can quickly achieve exit criteria through TDD.

list.item: Simplifies component-level test development
Improves code quality and structure
Improves testability
Easier to achieve desired code coverage
Reduces defect propagation to higher test levels
Improves cross-team communication
Takes more time initially to get accustomed
Improper TDD can result in false confidence in code quality

card.heading: Data-Driven Testing (DDT)
card.paragraph: Builds upon structured scripting. Test scripts are provided with test data from external sources (e.g., .csv, .xlsx, database dumps), allowing the same scripts to run multiple times with different data.

list.item: Quick and easy test case expansion through data feeds
Cost of adding new tests significantly reduced
Test analysts can specify tests via data files with less technical dependency
Proper test data management may be necessary

card.heading: Keyword-Driven Testing (KDT)
card.paragraph: Test cases are a list of test steps derived from keywords and test data. Keywords are defined from a user's perspective. Often built upon DDT.

list.item: Involves test analysts and business analysts in automated test creation
Can also be used for manual testing (ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119-5)
Implementing and maintaining keywords is complex, challenging at scale
Huge effort for smaller systems

card.heading: Behavior-Driven Development (BDD)
card.paragraph: Leverages a natural language format (Given / When / Then) in acceptance criteria stored as feature files. A BDD tool interprets the language and executes test cases.

list.item: Improves communication across teams
Automated BDD scenarios act as test cases ensuring specification coverage
Can produce multiple test types at different pyramid levels
Negative test conditions and edge cases still need separate definition
Teams often confuse BDD as only natural-language test writing
Maintaining natural language steps is complex
Overly complex steps make debugging difficult and costly

section.paragraph: Four major principles: encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, and polymorphism.
Single responsibility, Open-closed, Liskov substitution, Interface segregation, Dependency inversion. These improve code readability, maintainability, and scalability.

row.cell: Pattern
Purpose
Hides implementation details, exposing only what testers need for test cases
Ensures only one driver communicates with the SUT
Class file as page model; updates locators in one place when SUT structure changes
Double facade over page objects; stores user actions for improved abstraction and reuse

list.item: Use Page Object Model from day one — when a UI element changes, you fix one locator instead of fifty test scripts. The upfront cost pays for itself within weeks.
Keep layers thin and intentional — if your test scripts import directly from core libraries, your layering is broken. Enforce this with code reviews.
Start with structured scripting, graduate to DDT — once you have 20+ tests with similar flows but different data, externalize the data. CSV/JSON files are your friend.
Use BDD only when business stakeholders actually participate — if nobody reads the feature files, you're just adding a translation layer with no benefit.
Apply the facade pattern to hide driver complexity — your test writers should call loginAs("admin"), not chain 15 Selenium commands.
Reuse core libraries across projects — if you have two products on the same stack, shared core libraries cut your maintenance in half.
Review SOLID principles quarterly — test code rots faster than production code because it's reviewed less. Schedule refactoring sprints.
TAE-4.1.1 (K3) Apply guidelines for effective pilot and deployment activities
TAE-4.2.1 (K4) Analyze deployment risks and plan mitigation strategies
TAE-4.3.1 (K2) Explain factors affecting TAS maintainability

section.paragraph: A pilot project does not take long but its outcome significantly impacts project direction. Evaluate these to set up guidelines:

list.item: Programming language(s) to be used
Suitable commercial/open-source tools
Test levels to cover
Test cases selected
Test case development approach
Create multiple prototypes to show pros/cons of different approaches
Define timelines and periodically check progress
Integrate solution into CI/CD during pilot to expose issues early
Evaluate non-technical aspects: team knowledge, structure, licensing, planned test types
Evaluate effort after pilot completion to assess success/failure
Firewall openings
Resource utilization (CPU, RAM)
Network connection and reliability

row.cell: Risk Area
Considerations
Version control of testware; repository sharing across organization
Six levels: Fatal (abort execution), Error (test case fails), Warn (unexpected but non-breaking), Info (basic progress), Debug (execution-specific details for investigation), Trace (most granular)
Test harness, fixtures, preconditions/postconditions, suite grouping, atomicity. Fixtures enable repeatable and atomic tests
Automatic updates on harnesses/agents; version changes on devices

key-box.heading: Mobile Device Example
key-box.paragraph: Real devices for mobile test automation must be powered on, have enough battery, be connected to a network, and have access to the SUT. These are deployment risks that are not strictly test automation but directly affect its reliability.

section.paragraph: Follow clean code principles (Robert C. Martin). Maintainability is affected by programming standards and TAE expectations.

key-box.heading: Clean Code Principles for Test Automation

list.item: Use common naming conventions for meaningful names
Use logical and common project structure
Avoid hardcoding — use data-driven testing and constants
Avoid too many input parameters for methods
Avoid long, complex methods
Use logging
Use design patterns where beneficial
Focus on testability

section.paragraph: Use static analyzers and code formatters. Agree on a branching structure in version control (feature, release, and fix branches).

list.item: Always run a pilot before full rollout — pick 5-10 representative test cases, build them two different ways, compare. The 2-week pilot saves 6 months of rework.
Integrate into CI/CD during the pilot — don't wait until "the framework is ready." Early CI/CD integration catches environment, permission, and network issues before they compound.
Use meaningful log levels — Fatal/Error for failures, Info for progress, Debug for investigation. If your CI logs are all Info level, you'll drown in noise when something breaks.
Make tests atomic and repeatable — each test should set up its own preconditions and clean up after itself. Tests that depend on execution order are a maintenance nightmare.
Enforce naming conventions from day one — loginButton beats btn1 every time. Agree on conventions in your team's first sprint and lint for them.
Avoid hardcoding URLs, credentials, and timeouts — use environment variables or config files. The day you need to point tests at staging, you'll thank yourself.
Branch your test code like production code — feature branches for new tests, release branches for version alignment, hotfix branches for broken locators.
TAE-5.1.1 (K3) Apply test automation at different test levels within pipelines
TAE-5.1.2 (K2) Explain configuration management for testware
TAE-5.1.3 (K2) Explain test automation dependencies for an API infrastructure

section.paragraph: Automated tests run unattended, making them ideal for pipelines. Test levels are typically integrated as follows:

row.cell: Test Level
Pipeline Integration
During TAF/TAS build; verify paths and file existence
Part of build step; quality gates for CI pipeline
Part of CI if low-level; executed with component tests
Integrated into CD pipeline as last quality gate
Part of CD pipeline; ensures separately developed components work together

list.item: As part of deployment phase — tests can fail/rollback deployment, but reruns require redeployment

As separate pipeline — triggered by successful deployment; supports multiple test suites per deployment; requires manual rollback actions. Simple deployment check scripts verify the SUT is up but do not verify functional suitability broadly
Nightly regression — run regression suites periodically for longer-running tests
Non-functional tests — monitor performance efficiency and other quality characteristics

row.cell: Aspect
Details
URLs, credentials; stored with testware or in shared repository for multi-project use
Environment-specific or release-specific; stored with TAF or in test data management systems
Grouped by purpose (smoke, regression); feature toggles per release; versioned with SUT using tags/branches

section.paragraph: With shift left, it is recommended to support and divide API testing among different levels (component integration and system integration testing), extended with contract testing as a best practice.

list.item: API connections — understand business logic and relationships between APIs
API documentation — baseline for test automation (parameters, headers, request-response types)

key-box.heading: Contract Testing
key-box.paragraph: Integration testing that verifies services can communicate and that shared data is consistent with specified rules. Goes beyond schema validation — both parties agree on allowed interactions while allowing evolution. Defects from underlying services are found earlier in the SDLC.

list.item: Consumer-driven — consumer sets expectations for provider responses
Provider-driven — provider creates the contract showing how services operate
Put component tests in your build step — they're fast and catch issues before deployment. If your build step doesn't run tests, you're shipping blind.
Use feature toggles in test config — tag test suites per release so you only run relevant tests. No more "why are 200 tests running for a 2-file change?"
Version your testware with the SUT — use the same release tags/branches. When a production bug is reported on v3.2, you can checkout the exact testware that tested v3.2.
Add contract tests for microservices — they catch interface breaks before integration testing. Consumer-driven contracts are especially effective when services evolve independently.
Schedule nightly regression for long suites — if your full regression takes 4+ hours, don't block the pipeline. Run it nightly and review results at standup.
Separate environment config from test code — store URLs, credentials, and feature flags in config files or shared repositories, not in test scripts.
TAE-6.1.1 (K3) Apply data collection methods from TAS and SUT
TAE-6.1.2 (K4) Analyze data from TAS and SUT to understand test results
TAE-6.1.3 (K2) Explain how a test progress report is constructed and published
SUT logs — web/mobile UI, APIs, applications, web servers, database servers
TAF logs — audit trail
Production logs — performance monitoring, trend analysis, load/stress/spike testing
Screenshots and recordings — native or 3rd party
Current test case with start/end time
Execution status (passed, failed, TAS failure, inconclusive)
Low-level details including timing information
Dynamic SUT information (e.g., memory leaks)
Execution counters for reliability/stress testing
Random elements logged (parameters, choices)
All actions logged for replay capability
Screenshots for root cause analysis
Crash dumps and stack traces stored on failure

section.paragraph: Correlate TAS results with SUT logs to identify root causes. Log defect information (date/time, source location, error messages), configuration at startup, and synchronize logs with timestamps. Use correlation IDs / trace IDs to trace behavior across system interactions.

Differences between actual and expected results are not always clear. Tool support helps define comparisons that ignore expected differences (e.g., dates, timestamps) while highlighting unexpected ones. Use color to distinguish log types (defects in red, progress in green).

Test execution data can be exported for use in tracking and reporting tools (spreadsheets, XML, databases, report tools). This is often achieved through existing export formats or customized reporting output.
After execution, analyze results to identify failures in both SUT and TAS. TAS data is primary, SUT data is secondary.

list.item: Check if the same failure occurred in previous executions (known defect?)
Identify the test case and what it is testing
Find the test step where failure occurred
Analyze SUT state via screenshots, API/network logs
If SUT state is unexpected, log a defect with all necessary information

section.paragraph: When actual and expected results match but a failure was reported, the TAS likely contains a defect. Environment unavailability can cause seemingly real failures — check SUT logs for outages.
Must contain test results, SUT information, and test environment documentation in a format appropriate for stakeholders. Include execution history and responsible persons for failure investigation.

row.cell: Category
Typical Roles
Focus
Architect, project/delivery manager, test director
Trends, pass-fail ratios, reliability
Product owner, business representative, BA
Product use metrics
Team lead, TAE, tester, developer
Lower-level details

section.paragraph: Reports should be published to all relevant stakeholders — uploaded to a website or cloud, sent to mailing lists, or posted via chatbots. Keep a history of test reports to gather statistics about frequent regressions for trend analysis.

Modern tools aggregate data from pipelines, project management, and code repositories. Visualization using charts and colored icons (e.g., traffic lights) helps stakeholders see trends and make decisions. Trends include defect clusters, propagation to certain environments, SUT performance degradation, and build reliability. AI/ML tools help reduce time finding broken locators, analyzing failure reasons (SUT defect vs. TAS defect), and grouping common defects.

list.item: Log correlation IDs in every test — when a test fails, you can trace the exact request through every microservice. Without this, root cause analysis is guesswork.
Auto-capture screenshots on failure — a screenshot is worth a thousand log lines. Configure your framework to attach them to every failed test automatically.
Build a dashboard your manager can read — traffic-light status, pass/fail trends over time, top-5 flaky tests. If stakeholders can't self-serve, they'll keep asking you.
Define clear status categories — passed, failed, TAS failure, inconclusive. If "TAS failure" isn't a category, your pass rate will look worse than it is.
Keep report history for trend analysis — a single report tells you today's status. Six months of reports tell you if quality is improving or degrading.
Tailor reports per audience — management wants trends and risk, ops wants product metrics, technical teams want stack traces. One report doesn't fit all.
Ignore expected differences in assertions — configure your comparison tools to skip timestamps, UUIDs, and dynamic IDs. False failures from these waste everyone's time.
TAE-7.1.1 (K3) Plan to verify the test automation environment including tool setup
TAE-7.1.2 (K2) Explain correct behavior for automated test scripts/suites
TAE-7.1.3 (K2) Identify where test automation produces unexpected results
TAE-7.1.4 (K2) Explain how static analysis aids test automation code quality

row.cell: Area
Details
Configure executable components, libraries, data files; use automated installation or repository for consistency
Systematic approach to loading/unloading TAS; no discernible difference across environments; configuration management ensures reproducible setups
Verify access to internal/external systems and interfaces; check permissions for logging and reporting
Individual functional and non-functional testing of TAF components (object verification, logging accuracy, performance)

section.h2: 7.1.2 Correct Behavior of Automated Test Suites

list.item: Check composition — completeness (expected results present, test data available), correct TAF/SUT version
Verify new features — closely monitor first-time use of new TAF features
Repeatability — results should always be the same; move unreliable tests (e.g., race conditions) out of active suite
Intrusiveness — tight TAS-SUT coupling can cause failures not seen in production; developers may require manual reproduction of automation-identified failures to assist analysis

section.paragraph: When a test script fails or passes unexpectedly, perform root cause analysis: inspect test logs, performance data, setup/teardown. Execute isolated tests. The defect can be in the test case, SUT, TAF, hardware, or network. Verify all assertions are in place — missing assertions may result in inconclusive results.

section.h2: 7.1.4 Static Analysis for Test Automation Code
section.paragraph: Static code analysis finds vulnerabilities and defects in both SUT and TAF code. Benefits include:

list.item: Proactive defect detection early in the SDLC via pipelines
Severity categorization (critical, high, medium, low) for prioritization
Suggested code fixes for identified defects
Security violation detection (e.g., plaintext passwords in test scripts)
Code quality measurement, commenting suggestions, and resource handling improvements

section.paragraph: Extend scanning best practices to test automation code — even though it is not deployed with the software, vulnerabilities like exposed credentials are a risk.

list.item: Verify your TAS setup before running tests — a 5-minute connectivity check (can the TAS reach the SUT? are permissions set? are logs writable?) prevents hours of "why is everything failing?" debugging.
Quarantine flaky tests immediately — move unreliable tests out of the active suite and investigate separately. One flaky test erodes trust in the entire suite.
Run static analysis on your test code — it catches plaintext passwords, unused imports, and code smells. Your test code is code too; treat it that way.
Check every assertion is in place — a test that passes without asserting anything is worse than no test. It gives false confidence. Audit your suite for "empty" tests quarterly.
Automate your TAS setup/teardown — use Docker, scripts, or config management to ensure identical environments. If setup is manual, it will drift between runs.
When a test passes unexpectedly, investigate — unexpected passes can mean missing assertions, stale test data, or a SUT change that made the test irrelevant.
TAE-8.1.1 (K3) Discover opportunities for improving test cases through data collection and analysis
TAE-8.1.2 (K4) Analyze technical aspects of a deployed TAS and provide recommendations
TAE-8.1.3 (K3) Restructure automated testware to align with SUT updates
TAE-8.1.4 (K2) Summarize opportunities for use of test automation tools

section.paragraph: Visual report of test data showing improvement areas regarding test case data trends. Enables TAEs to identify fragile test cases for refactoring.
AI/ML tools detect changed UI locators and use self-healing algorithms to fix test cases, speeding up version control changes and code maintenance.

Applied to API and database analysis. Checks if responses match business specifications (mandatory elements, object types). Reduces the need for individual assertions, making test code shorter and more efficient at detecting backend defects.

Example: An API has six mandatory response elements that must be strings. With schema validation tools, individual assertions for each type and null-check are unnecessary — the schema validation handles all checks automatically.

row.cell: Area
Improvement Opportunities
Upgrade scripting technique; consolidate overlapping test steps into library functions; establish failure recovery; evaluate wait mechanisms (hard-coded vs. polling vs. event subscription)
Concurrent execution on multiple environments; split long suites; remove duplication; parallel batch jobs in CI/CD
Standard verification methods for all tests; parameterization for similar methods
Change architecture to support SUT testability improvements; expensive but major improvement
Pilot and impact analysis before adopting new core library versions; create adoption plan per team
Move repeated actions into setup/teardown methods; use web service calls for preconditions/postconditions
Test automation docs, user docs, test reports and logs
Add only features that will be used; unused features increase complexity
Test latest versions with representative sample tests before rollout

list.item: Hard-coded waits — root cause of many defects due to unpredictable response times
Dynamic polling — waits only needed time; include timeout to prevent infinite waiting
Event subscription — most reliable; requires language support and SUT event offering; timeout still required
Identify environment changes — evaluate impact on testware, libraries, OS; make changes incrementally with MVP mindset
Improve core function libraries — incorporate new techniques (optimized code, newer OS libraries) for current and future projects
Consolidate control functions — merge specialized functions acting on same control types into fewer, general functions
Refactor TAA — evolve architecture with SUT; avoid bolt-on implementations
Naming conventions — keep consistent with previously defined standards
Evaluate existing scripts — decompose complex tests; eliminate infrequently-used tests

section.paragraph: Beyond testing, automation tools can assist with:

row.cell: Activity
How Automation Helps
Automated user creation via web service endpoints; infrastructure setup and cleanup scripts
Manipulate test data (e.g., keep date fields current)
Built-in tool capabilities for release documentation or marketing purposes

list.item: Review your test histogram monthly — identify the top-10 flakiest tests and refactor them. Fragile tests cost more in investigation time than they save in execution.
Replace hard-coded waits with dynamic polling — sleep(5000) is the #1 cause of flaky tests. Use explicit waits with timeouts instead. Your suite will be faster and more reliable.
Consolidate duplicate test steps into library functions — if three tests all "log in as admin," that's one function, not three copies. When the login flow changes, you fix one place.
Use schema validation for API tests — one schema check replaces 20 individual assertions. When the API contract changes, the schema tells you exactly what broke.
Make changes incrementally — when restructuring testware, deploy to a small subset first. Run a limited set of tests to verify no side effects before full rollout.
Leverage automation for non-test tasks — use your test scripts to set up environments, create test users, age data, and clean up. This saves hours of manual prep per sprint.
Pilot new library versions before adopting — run representative sample tests against the new version. A "minor" update that breaks your locator strategy is not minor.
Eliminate dead tests — if a test hasn't run in 6 months or tests a removed feature, delete it. A smaller, focused suite is faster and easier to maintain.

toc.item: Ch 1: Introduction and Objectives for Test Automation
Ch 6: Test Automation Reporting and Metrics
Ch 7: Verifying the Test Automation Solution

syllabus.footer-text: ISTQB CTAL-TAE v2.0 (2024) — Test Automation Engineering Study Guide
